journal_entry.description: Board declared cash dividend payable in 30 days
journal_entry.rationale: When a dividend is declared, it creates a legal obligation to pay. Under IFRS, we debit Dividends (or Retained Earnings, depending on account structure) to reduce equity, and credit Dividends Payable to recognize the liability. The dividend will be paid in 30 days. The debit tuple indicates one dividend increase (slot b=1), and the credit tuple indicates one liability increase (slot a=1), which aligns with the standard accounting treatment for dividend declaration. No tax applies to dividend declarations.

output.reason: When a cash dividend is declared, the credit side creates a liability (dividend payable) since the company now owes the dividend to shareholders. This is a liability increase. The dividend account itself (owner withdrawal) is debited, not credited.

credit_classifier.reason: When a cash dividend is declared, the credit side creates a liability (dividend payable) since the company now owes the dividend to shareholders. This is a liability increase. The dividend account itself (owner withdrawal) is debited, not credited.

output.reason: When a board declares a cash dividend, the debit side records the dividend increase (distribution to owners). This is a dividend declaration, not yet paid, so we debit Dividends (or Retained Earnings) = 1 dividend increase. The credit side (not classified here) would increase Dividends Payable liability.

debit_classifier.reason: When a board declares a cash dividend, the debit side records the dividend increase (distribution to owners). This is a dividend declaration, not yet paid, so we debit Dividends (or Retained Earnings) = 1 dividend increase. The credit side (not classified here) would increase Dividends Payable liability.
